criteria: TAP device successfully opens with IFF_TAP | IFF_NO_PI flags and can be configured with an IP address and netmask via ioctl SIOCSIFADDR and SIOCSIFNETMASK
tap_read() and tap_write() functions correctly receive and transmit raw Ethernet frames with correct byte counts matching the frame length
Ethernet frame parser extracts 6-byte destination MAC, 6-byte source MAC, and 2-byte EtherType field with correct offsets (bytes 0-5, 6-11, 12-13 respectively)
Parser correctly detects IEEE 802.1Q VLAN-tagged frames (EtherType 0x8100), extracts the 4-byte VLAN tag, identifies the inner EtherType, and adjusts payload offset by 4 bytes
All multi-byte Ethernet and ARP fields (EtherType, ARP hw_type, proto_type, opcode) are converted using ntohs()/ntohl() on receive and htons()/htonl() on transmit
ARP request handler verifies hw_type=1 (Ethernet), proto_type=0x0800 (IPv4), and hw_addr_len/proto_addr_len match expected values before processing
ARP reply is sent with correct sender_hw=our_mac, sender_proto=our_ip, target_hw=requester_mac, target_proto=requester_ip when target_ip matches our configured IP
ARP cache implements hash-based lookup using IP address as key and returns cached MAC entries within the TTL window (default 60 seconds)
Expired ARP cache entries are invalidated on lookup and removed from the cache
Pending packet queue buffers outbound Ethernet frames when destination MAC is unknown, with configurable maximum queue depth per IP address
ARP resolution triggers a broadcast request (destination MAC FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) for unknown IPs and queues the pending packet
When ARP reply is received for a pending IP, all queued packets are updated with the resolved destination MAC and transmitted
Broadcast Ethernet frames (destination MAC FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) are accepted and processed, not filtered out
Main event loop uses poll() or select() to wait for frame availability with non-blocking timeout for periodic tasks
All protocol structures use __attribute__((packed)) to prevent compiler padding from breaking wire format alignment

IPv4 parser correctly extracts version (must be 4), IHL (multiplied by 4 for byte offset), total length, identification, flags (DF, MF), fragment offset (multiplied by 8 for bytes), TTL, protocol, source IP, and destination IP with proper network-to-host byte order conversion

IP header checksum is computed using RFC 1071 ones' complement sum algorithm and verified on every received packet; packets with invalid checksums are silently dropped without generating ICMP error messages

ICMP echo reply (Type 0, Code 0) is sent with matching identifier and sequence number in response to incoming echo request (Type 8, Code 0); ping responds within 10ms on local network with correct ICMP checksum covering the entire message

IP fragmentation reassembly correctly reconstructs original datagram from fragments using (src_ip, dst_ip, id, protocol) tuple as key; fragments are stored by offset in a sorted linked list; incomplete reassemblies are discarded after 30-second timeout with periodic cleanup

Routing table supports static route entries with (network, prefix_len, gateway, src_ip, interface, metric) and selects the correct next-hop gateway using longest prefix match algorithm with metric as tiebreaker

TTL is decremented on forwarded packets BEFORE routing lookup; packets with TTL reaching 0 or 1 generate ICMP Time Exceeded (Type 11, Code 0) sent back to source IP with original IP header + first 8 bytes of payload included

Packets destined for configured local IP addresses are delivered to upper-layer protocol handlers (ICMP, TCP, UDP) based on protocol field; unknown protocols trigger ICMP Destination Unreachable (Type 3, Code 2)
ICMP checksum covers entire ICMP message including data payload; ICMP error messages include the original IP header plus first 8 bytes of the original payload per RFC 792
Fragment reassembly handles out-of-order arrival and duplicate fragment detection; overlapping fragments are handled by preferring first-received data
IP parser rejects packets with version != 4, IHL < 5 or IHL > 15, total_len < header_len, and truncated packets where buffer length < total_len

TCP segment parser correctly extracts source port, destination port, sequence number, acknowledgment number, data offset, all 6 standard flags (SYN/ACK/FIN/RST/PSH/URG), window size, checksum, and urgent pointer from raw network data
TCP checksum verification uses the pseudo-header (source IP, destination IP, zero byte, protocol 6, TCP length) plus the complete TCP segment; segments with invalid checksums are silently dropped
3-way handshake completes correctly with server transitioning LISTEN → SYN_RECEIVED → ESTABLISHED and client transitioning CLOSED → SYN_SENT → ESTABLISHED
MSS option is correctly parsed from SYN and SYN-ACK segments during connection establishment; the negotiated MSS limits outbound segment payload size with a default of 536 bytes when option is absent

Full 11-state TCP state machine (CLOSED, LISTEN, SYN_SENT, SYN_RECEIVED, ESTABLISHED, FIN_WAIT_1, FIN_WAIT_2, CLOSE_WAIT, CLOSING, LAST_ACK, TIME_WAIT) implements RFC 793 transitions including simultaneous open and simultaneous close
RST segments are handled correctly based on connection state — received RST aborts the connection immediately; RST is generated in response to segments for non-existent connections or invalid states
4-way FIN handshake performs graceful connection teardown with the active closer entering TIME_WAIT for 2*MSL (60 seconds default) to absorb any delayed segments from the network
Connection table correctly manages multiple concurrent TCP sessions keyed by the 4-tuple (local IP, local port, remote IP, remote port)
Sequence number comparisons use modulo 2^32 arithmetic with SEQ_LT/SEQ_LE/SEQ_GT/SEQ_GE macros to handle wraparound correctly
Initial Sequence Numbers (ISN) are generated using time-based or hash-based algorithm per RFC 6528 to prevent sequence number prediction attacks
SYN and FIN flags correctly consume one sequence number each in the sequence space
TCP options parsing correctly handles EOL, NOP, MSS, Window Scale, and SACK Permitted options with proper length validation
Sender sliding window tracks SND.UNA (oldest unacked) and SND.NXT (next to send), allowing transmission of up to min(cwnd_bytes, rwnd) - (SND.NXT - SND.UNA) bytes before blocking
Receiver advertised window (rwnd) extracted from all incoming ACKs and strictly enforced; sender never allows bytes in flight to exceed rwnd
Out-of-order segments detected via sequence number comparison (SEQ_GT) and buffered in OOO buffer structure, then delivered to receive buffer contiguously when gaps are filled
RTO calculated using Jacobson's algorithm with SRTT and RTTVAR variables per RFC 6298, initialized to 1 second minimum
Karn's algorithm implemented: RTT samples from retransmitted segments (detected via retransmitted_seq tracking) are not used for RTO calculation
Retransmission triggered on RTO expiration with exponential backoff (RTO *= 2) on successive timeouts for same segment range
Slow start increases cwnd by 1 MSS per received ACK (exponential growth per RTT) when cwnd < ssthresh
Congestion avoidance increases cwnd by MSS*(MSS/cwnd) per ACK (approximately 1 MSS per RTT linear growth) when cwnd >= ssthresh
Fast retransmit triggers on receipt of 3rd duplicate ACK (dup_ack_count >= 3), immediately retransmitting segment at SND.UNA without waiting for RTO
Fast recovery sets ssthresh = cwnd/2 and cwnd = ssthresh + 3*MSS after fast retransmit, inflates cwnd by 1 MSS per additional duplicate ACK, exits when ACK covers recover_seq
End-to-end 1MB file transfer completes with SHA-256 verification matching original file, demonstrating correct in-order delivery over unreliable packet network
Raw socket or TAP device successfully receives and sends Ethernet frames on the network interface
Ethernet parser correctly extracts 6-byte source MAC, 6-byte destination MAC, and 2-byte EtherType field
Parser correctly handles IEEE 802.1Q VLAN-tagged frames by detecting EtherType 0x8100 and skipping the 4-byte tag
ARP reply with correct hardware and protocol addresses is sent when an ARP request matches our configured IP
ARP table caches resolved entries and evicts stale entries after a configurable timeout (default 60 seconds)
ARP request is sent for unknown destination IPs, and outbound packets are queued until resolution completes or times out
All multi-byte fields are converted between network byte order (big-endian) and host byte order using explicit conversion functions
TCP segment parser correctly extracts source port, destination port, sequence number, acknowledgment number, data offset, flags (SYN/ACK/FIN/RST/PSH), window size, checksum, and urgent pointer
TCP checksum is computed over the pseudo-header (source IP, destination IP, zero byte, protocol 6, TCP length) plus the TCP segment; segments with invalid checksums are dropped
3-way handshake completes successfully — server transitions LISTEN→SYN_RECEIVED→ESTABLISHED; client transitions CLOSED→SYN_SENT→ESTABLISHED
MSS option is parsed from SYN/SYN-ACK segments and used to limit outbound segment payload size; default MSS of 536 bytes is used when option is absent
State machine implements all 11 TCP states from RFC 793 with correct transitions, including simultaneous open and simultaneous close
RST segments are handled correctly — received RST aborts the connection; RST is sent in response to segments received in invalid states
4-way FIN handshake gracefully closes connection; the closing side enters TIME_WAIT for 2*MSL (default 60 seconds) to absorb delayed segments
Connection table supports multiple concurrent connections keyed by (local IP, local port, remote IP, remote port) tuple
Sequence number comparisons use modulo 2^32 arithmetic (SEQ_LT, SEQ_GT macros) to handle wraparound correctly
ISN generation is cryptographically unpredictable per RFC 6528, using time-based and connection-tuple hashing
TCP pseudo-header checksum correctly includes IP addresses from the IP layer, breaking clean layer separation as required by RFC 793
Performance targets met: TCP parsing <500ns, checksum <300ns, connection lookup <1μs, state transition <100ns, ISN generation <1μs
